tweets.full_text:
AIで副業自動化したいって言いながら、実は「完成した仕組みを眺めてニヤニヤしたい欲」だけの人、めちゃくちゃ多くない？

俺も最初そうだった。Claude Codeで投稿を自動化した時、できた瞬間に誰にも頼まれてないのにX（Twitter）で報告したくてウズウズしてた。

でも気づいたんだ。その先の「稼ぐ」「時間を減らす」には、仕組みができた後が本当の勝負なんだ。

完成後も毎日調整が必要。データ見て、反応悪い投稿ネタは削除して、新しいパターン追加して。これの繰り返し。

クラシコとか新しいAIが出ても、それに頼るんじゃなくて、自分のスプレッドシートに「このネタはいつウケた」「このタイミングで投稿すると反応が来る」っていう過去データを溜め込んでおく。

それが本当の自動化だ。

仕組みを眺めるだけなら、誰でも1日でできる。

稼ぎ続けるには、退屈な作業を続ける覚悟が要る。

tweets.full_text:
Claude Codeで作ったLPから問い合わせ50件超えた事例が出てきてて、AI経営の次のフェーズに入ったなと

@masahirochaenが法人LPからブログ自動生成、SEO改善まで全部Claude Codeで組んでて

①LP制作はデザインからコーディングまでAI完結
外注なら50-100万かかるところを数時間で

②ブログ記事を自動生成してSEO流入を確保
Search Consoleのデータを読み込ませて改善サイクルを回してる

③SEOで2位まで上がってるらしい
広告費ゼロでオーガニック集客だけで回してる

うちもClaude Codeで同じことやってるけど、ポイントは作って終わりじゃなくてデータを食わせて回し続ける設計にすること。月$200のツールでLP+SEOが回るのは普通にコスパが壊れてる

tweets.full_text:
📋 Daily Intel 5/11 朝｜Anthropic エンジニアが Claude Code で Polymarket bot 構築・$200→$14,300／Claude モバイルアプリ全体を構築可能に／ChatGPT「世代別の使われ方」が分岐／Pakistan が ICP の sovereign cloud で AI-native 政府インフラ／Midnight が AI agent にプライバシー基盤

■ Anthropic エンジニアが Claude を使って Polymarket で $200 → $14,300
Anthropic のエンジニアが Claude Code で bot を構築、8,600万件のトレードを分析してプラットフォーム全体で最も収益性の高い wallet を発見した（@igus_ai 5/10 15:12）。AI を「自分の代わりに調査・判断・取引する agent」として一般人が運用できる範囲が一気に広がった事例。AI agent commerce の文脈で「賭け／投資／予測市場」が早期に AI 委譲領域になる兆し。あなたが next の判断を AI に任せる場面はどこ？

■ 「Claude が今やあなたのモバイルアプリ全体を構築可能 — $350K の Apple レベル開発者のように」
これまでフルチームが数週間（数千ドル）かかった作業が、数回のプロンプトで実現する範囲に入った（@WealthEmpireHQ 5/10 05:22）。「app を持つ」コストが個人の手の届く位置に。あなたは何のアプリを「自分のために」作る？

■ Sam Altman: ChatGPT「世代別の使われ方」が分岐
@Cointelegraph 経由：シニア層は Google のように／20-30代は「life advisor」として／大学生は「OS」として — 自分のファイル・プロンプト・日常判断と接続して使う（5/10 15:30）。AI が「外部頭脳」「相棒」「OS」のどれになるかは年代差ではなく、向き合い方の差。あなたの ChatGPT 関係は、これからどの形に進化させたい？

■ Pakistan ICP sovereign cloud subnet が mainnet LIVE
Pakistan Digital Authority と DFINITY が「Pakistan Sovereign $ICP Cloud Subnet」稼働開始。AI-native な政府インフラ、Web3 ベースの信用層、DID と新しいデジタル法の枠組みを同時に積む（@DfinityToday 5/10 18:54）。「国家がクラウドを sovereign に持つ」未来が pilot phase を超えてきた。

■ IOG「Midnight + Midnight Passport が AI agent にプライバシー基盤＋標準を持ち込む」
@IOGroup（5/8 BM）。Stripe @link wallet for agents が AI が代理で買い物する layer なら、Midnight は AI agent が credentials/identity を「漏らさず」やり取りする layer。ZK + DID で agent-to-agent commerce のプライバシー regime が動き出している。あなたのデータが AI 経由でどこまで露出するか、選べる時代の入口。

■ 注目の周辺
・@elonmusk 異例の「無税パーティー」スタイル投稿が 50万 likes 超（5/10 05:07） — マスク氏のテンション維持の文化的シグナル。
・@1x_tech NEO 家庭用ヒューマノイド「The Home Robot Order Today」、累計 6.8万 likes — 個人が予約できる home robot の地平が現実。
・@Filecoin × @Cardano（Charles + Marta Belcher）対談、Filecoin Onchain で（4/29 BM） — Cardano が EiB スケールの分散ストレージを利用するナラティブ。

■ 日本側ライフ視座
@Pre_Online: 「日本円の紙くず化カウントダウン｜高市首相が狙う『借金1342兆円帳消し』禁断の処方箋」（5/6 BM）— ポピュリズム/MMT/PB 議論として日本社会で確実に拡散している話法。あなたの円建て資産・収入・将来計画への影響をどう設計する？

■ 一次ソース:

tweets.full_text:
I've been working on a Trading View pine script algo the past few weeks. Using Claude Code to assist with adjustments and writing the code. I set alerts for the script in Trading View and a webhook gets sent to TradersPost which triggers orders on Coinbase via API. 

The script is dialed in enough to run live(imo), and I'm currently live with small orders to test signal quality. First order, trigger to completion, went through in 1.11 seconds with nearly zero slippage. Waiting for more. 

If anyone reading this post has any experience or knowledge of this process, I would love some feedback. This is my first time attempting to algo trade, so I'm a novice. I'll post updates of my journey if you want to follow.

Below I attached screenshots of a few trade setups, and results of my Monte Carlo test.

-Light blue tags are entries
-Darker blue tags are DCA entries
-Green tags are entry signals that were skipped due to being in position already 
-Red tags are successful exits
-Grey tags are exit signals, but with zero position to exit
-Orange tags are entries blocked by filter

Monte carlo test:

- 100% probability of profit — never lost money in any of 10,000 shuffles                      
  - Worst case ever: +67.43% — even the unluckiest trade ordering still returns +67%
  - Median return: +107.5%, median DD: 1.3%                                                      
  - 0% chance of DD > 10% — max DD in worst sim was 4.64%   
  - Median Ret/DD: 79.61 — absurdly high risk-adjusted return

  The DCA with fixed cash sizing is extremely robust. The trade pool of 264 trades is large
  enough that reshuffling can't produce a losing outcome. The narrow return band (85% to 130% at
  5th/95th percentile) shows low variance too.

tweets.full_text: @Priyannkaaaa LC into LLM and building with claude code currently built web apps moving towards agnetic stuff

Someone asked r/ProductManagement if anyone uses Claude Code for strategy work.

40 comments of pure fury.

Junior PM: I read that thread and I can't sleep. If AI can do strategy, what am I even here for?

Senior PM: Which part of strategy are you worried about losing?

Junior PM: Competitive analysis, opportunity sizing, writing PRDs. AI handles all of that now.

Senior PM: Try an exercise. Open Claude right now and ask it to prioritize your Q3 roadmap.

Junior PM: I just tried it and it gave me a decent framework with six criteria and a scoring matrix.

Senior PM: Does that output match what your team should build next quarter?

Junior PM: It doesn't, because it has no idea our CTO wants to migrate platforms, or that our biggest customer threatened to churn last week, or that we only have two senior engineers until September.

Senior PM: So the gap between that output and a real strategy is the 50 things you carry in your head about your company, your team, and your users.

Junior PM: Right, it's the context. The team dynamics, the technical debt, what our CEO said about shifting focus in the last all-hands. None of that made it into the prompt.

Senior PM: When people in that Reddit thread said "sure, sloppify the most important part of the job," I think they were reacting to PMs who paste a vague prompt and treat the output as strategy. And they're right to be frustrated by that.

Junior PM: So should I refuse AI and go back to doing quarterly planning manually over three weeks?

Senior PM: You could, and most of that thread would agree with you. But here's the exercise I'd suggest instead. Step one: write down your company's top three goals for the year in one page. Step two: list your five biggest product constraints, things like technical debt, team size, platform dependencies. Step three: document what your last ten user interviews told you about the biggest pain points. Those three documents take maybe two hours to write. Then feed them to AI alongside every strategic question.

Junior PM: So instead of AI replacing my judgment, it runs on top of my judgment because it has my context.

Senior PM: And the output goes from generic consulting-firm advice to recommendations that already account for your engineering constraints and your CEO's priorities. I think most PMs could cut their quarterly planning from three weeks of data gathering down to four or five days of making judgment calls, because the synthesis part is handled.

Junior PM: That's the opposite of what the Reddit thread assumes people are doing with AI.

Senior PM: I think both camps are wrong. The refusers are leaving a 3x to 5x speed multiplier on the table. The blind delegators are producing generic slop that any intern could spot. The PMs building context systems right now, the ones documenting their company knowledge in structured files and feeding it to every AI interaction, those are the ones who will have a clear edge in 12 months. Your competitive advantage was never analysis speed. It was always judgment applied to context that only you have.

That context system is what PM OS gives you.

Now V2 comes packed with a memory system influenced heavily by Hermes and OpenClaw agents.

Takes about 15 minutes to set everything up, and it cuts strategic planning time by 80% going forward.

→ https://t.co/7HiQNAK8xr

tweets.full_text:
This Chinese guy built agents in Claude Code for cold email campaigns and single-handedly serves 38 B2B businesses a month, taking $3000 from each.

He built a system of 7 agents on Claude Sonnet 4.6 that scrapes LinkedIn and Apollo in narrow verticals, finds B2B companies with broken pipelines, and over 1 weekend takes each one to a finished sample campaign with a personalized Loom and cold message.

No VA, no sales team, no SDR. Just him, a MacBook, an iPhone, and 1 API key.

And traditional cold email agencies keep teams of 9 people on salary for the same order flow, while his expenses are only tokens and subscriptions to Smartlead, Higgsfield, and Calendly.

7 agents work through 1 orchestrator on Claude Code Router. Usage is about 3.4 million tokens a day, the average API bill is about $540 a month.

All 7 go through MCP servers and write shared state to the file system, without shared state in memory and without race conditions, and 1 of them lives right in the iPhone and picks up positive replies from the subway, a taxi, or on walks.

And here is the system prompt he put into the orchestrator before launch:
"You are the orchestrator of a solo agency that sells done-for-you cold email campaigns to B2B businesses. You delegate read-only tasks to 6 sub-agents and own all writes.

sub-agents:

// Scout (sweeps LinkedIn, Apollo, and job boards in selected verticals: 4+ years in business, actively hiring SDRs or BDRs, no outbound footprint or last campaign from 2023, but solid revenue signals)

// Diagnoser (for each lead writes a 50-word pipeline diagnosis, hero angle, tone matched to the vertical, and a cold message under 70 words)

// Builder (generates a sample 5-step sequence + 50 verified prospects in Smartlead through MCP only for the top 4 leads per day, with the sharpest diagnoses and the biggest pipeline gap)

// Filmer (pulls 6 screenshots of the campaign mockup and through Higgsfield renders a 45-second personalized Loom-style walkthrough with the prospect's logo on screen)

// Pitcher (sends a personalized cold message through the right channel for the vertical: email to SaaS founders, LinkedIn to consultancies and M&A shops, SMS to logistics ops leads, IG DM to ecom brands)

// Checker (runs every message through evals for personalization, absence of AI markers and buzzwords before sending)

// Mobile (lives in the iPhone, handles positive replies in real time, books Zoom calls in Calendly through MCP while the owner is on the go).
You never let 2 sub-agents touch 1 lead. You stop and request approval from the human only when a deal exceeds $4,000 or the reply rate in a vertical for the day drops below 11%."

Meaning the system knows what it is and within what boundaries it is allowed to act.

It knows it is supposed to find leads on its own.

It knows it is supposed to take each one to a sample campaign, Loom, and cold message without intervention.

It knows the human only steps in when a deal goes above $4,000 or the reply rate stops converging.

→ The system runs 24 hours a day

→ Scout sweeps about 240 B2B companies across LinkedIn and Apollo per day and leaves 32 new leads in the queue

→ Diagnoser outputs 32 structured diagnoses + briefs + cold messages per day

→ Builder assembles 3 to 4 finished sample campaigns in Smartlead for the sharpest leads

→ Filmer renders a 45-second personalized Loom in Higgsfield for each one

→ Pitcher sends 32 personalized messages per day across 4 channels with a reply rate of about 16%

→ Checker runs every message through evals before sending
And only when a deal breaks $4,000 or the reply rate for the day drops below 11% does the orchestrator wake the owner.

And when the owner at that moment is sitting in the subway or a taxi, the Mobile agent in his iPhone picks up 1 move on its own: replies to a fresh positive reply from a SaaS founder, books a Zoom through Calendly synced to the local time of the client, and puts the lead back in the queue.

The owner only has to tap "approve" and in just 10 minutes join the call.

Here is what the system writes in his log during 1 of the Saturdays:

"scout report: 244 B2B companies checked across SaaS, healthtech, and logistics, 38 actively hiring SDRs, 21 with no outbound footprint, 7 publicly complaining about pipeline on LinkedIn. passing top 32 to diagnoser."

"pitcher: 32 cold messages sent across 4 channels, 16 replies, 6 positive, 4 Zoom calls booked for Sunday. passing to closer."

"builder: sample campaign for Ridgeway Logistics built in Instantly, 3-step sequence, 50 verified prospects, consultative tone. URL placed at /Users/dev/cold-stack/clients/ridgeway/v1. filmer launching Higgsfield."

"eval flag: deal with Halcyon Capital Partners at $4,200 exceeds the approved limit of $4,000. sending for manual review."

He has no server of his own and no separate backend.

Just a local file sandbox at /Users/dev/cold-stack, an MCP router, 1 API key to Claude, and the same key forwarded to Claude Code on his iPhone.

Out of everything I have seen this year, this is the cleanest one-person agency for selling cold email to B2B businesses: $540 a month on the API, about $19,000 into the account, and between them 7 prompts, 1 file system, and 1 phone in the pocket.

tweets.full_text:
Sure, Claude Code can do this, hovewer once you've manually added the https://t.co/H7pxPfVLxw MCP server and authenticated via OAuth to your Financial Datasets account. Free tier covers basics, KPI/Pro data is paid. 

As far as I know Opus 4.7 has zero native access to live market data. The breakthrough here is MCP + a licensed data provider, not the model itself. Right?

Without that, Claude can technically scrape analyst estimates via web_search + web_fetch, but you're burning 50k+ tokens per lookup, dealing with paywalls, and trusting the model to parse unstructured HTML without hallucinating numbers or reading old data.

tweets.full_text:
The new iai-mcp project (https://t.co/OgRMYPawAa, MIT, 4 days old) ships with a long-term-memory architecture for Claude Code and a publishable benchmark harness. Their README badge advertises ≥99% verbatim recall at 10k records. I wanted to know how it compares to mempalace, which I've been running for months and which already publishes a LongMemEval-S R@5 of 96.6% in raw mode.

Ran iai-mcp's own bench.longmemeval_blind on the full 500-question LongMemEval-S split (xiaowu0162/longmemeval-cleaned @ 98d7416c). Scored mempalace from its existing per-question JSONL artifacts against the same gold session IDs from the dataset.

Setup

git clone https://t.co/HzXIr4dhAe
cd iai-mcp && python3.12 -m venv .venv && .venv/bin/pip install -e .
rm -f /tmp/out.json /tmp/out.json.jsonl
IAI_MCP_CRYPTO_PASSPHRASE='whatever' \
  .venv/bin/python -m bench.longmemeval_blind --split S --out /tmp/out.json
Two iai-mcp configs run (default bge-small-en-v1.5, then matched all-MiniLM-L6-v2 to remove the embedder confound). mempalace's published JSONL results re-scored against the HF gold for apples-to-apples.
~38 min per 500-row run on M-series. Per-row tmp lancedb stores get cleaned automatically.
Headline numbers
SystemNR@5R@10iai-mcp default (bge-small)5000.96200.9780iai-mcp matched (MiniLM-L6)5000.96600.9820mempalace raw (MiniLM)5000.96600.9820mempalace hybrid_v4 + Haiku rerank5000.99201.0000
95% Wilson CI at p=0.966, n=500: ±1.27pp. At p=0.992: ±0.49pp.

iai-mcp matched and mempalace raw aren't just close — they tied to four decimal places. So I broke it down by question type:

Per-type R@5
typeiai defaultiai matchedmempal rawmempal hybrid+Haikuknowledge-update0.9871.0001.0001.000multi-session0.9850.9920.9920.992single-session-assistant0.9640.9640.9641.000single-session-preference0.8670.9670.9670.967single-session-user0.9570.9140.9141.000temporal-reasoning0.9470.9470.9470.985
iai-mcp matched and mempalace raw are identical on every single category. They're retrieving the same sessions in the same order.

What I read from this
On retrieval: iai-mcp's runtime graph + activation cascade does not change ordering vs plain ChromaDB on this benchmark. Same embedder in, same ranks out. The ML machinery isn't earning its keep on LongMemEval-S.
On the embedder choice: iai-mcp ships bge-small as default. On this dataset bge-small is worse than MiniLM-L6 by 0.4pp on both metrics, driven almost entirely by a 10-point dip on single-session-preference. Counterintuitive but reproducible.
On the published 99% verbatim claim: that's a different benchmark (verbatim string recall at 10k inserted records, not LongMemEval). I didn't run it. The ≥99% may well hold — but it's not a retrieval-quality property, and people reading the README badge in the context of "memory for AI assistants" may conflate the two.

On mempalace hybrid_v4 + Haiku: clear winner here. 2.6pp R@5 lead over both raw configs (well outside CI), 100% R@10 across every category. Costs ~$0.001/query.

Caveats
Single benchmark. LongMemEval-S leans on retrieval recall; doesn't stress write throughput, encryption, daemon lifecycle, sleep-cycle consolidation, or any of iai-mcp's other claims.
N=500 is sufficient for this finding (CI ±1.27pp, observed delta vs hybrid is 2.6pp). It is not sufficient to make sub-percentage claims.
Both systems run on default ChromaDB / LanceDB stacks. A different vector index would shift numbers.
The 50-question subset in early posts about iai-mcp showed iai-mcp leading by 2pp R@5. That gap closed at full N. Don't trust 50q numbers.

Two practical gotchas if you run this yourself
Crypto trap. iai-mcp's bench silently reports R@5=0.000, errors=500 if IAI_MCP_CRYPTO_PASSPHRASE isn't set or iai-mcp crypto init wasn't run first. Errors are counted as MISS, not skipped, so you get a clean-looking JSON saying you scored zero. Cost me one wasted run.
Resume trap. Harness checkpoints to <out>.jsonl and resumes on next invocation. After a failed run you must rm -f <out>.jsonl or the next run will just re-emit the failures.

tweets.full_text: @OskarKohler the 'skills that overlap' one is the sneakiest. most people don't notice until they trace which skill the agent actually picked. registries catch this. just shipped one for Claude Code + OpenClaw via clawhub: https://t.co/YHKeTGndjq

4/ Tested today on dotbro¹ with a Claude Code skill that lives at both `~/.claude/skills/X` and `~/.agents/skills/X`. Same trick works for stow, chezmoi — anything that calls symlink(2) without resolving src first.

¹ https://t.co/m04xwwlH7r

/end

tweets.full_text:
𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗽𝗮𝗿𝘁: 𝗸𝗲𝗲𝗽𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴.

Claude code exits after each task. so I built the ralph Wiggum stop hook, re-injects the prompt, loops back until the file lands in Done/.

agent engineering. not prompt engineering.
